question.e1: I am the life of the party.
question.a1: I feel little concern for others.
question.n2: I am relaxed most of the time.
question.o2: I have difficulty understanding abstract ideas.
question.c4: I make a mess of things.
question.o4: I am not interested in abstract ideas.
question.a5: I am not interested in other people's problems.
question.c5: I get chores done right away.
question.c6: I often forget to put things back in their proper place.
question.o6: I do not have a good imagination.
question.e7: I talk to a lot of different people at parties.
question.a7: I am not really interested in others.
question.n7: I change my mood a lot.
question.o7: I am quick to understand things.
question.e8: I don't like to draw attention to myself.
question.a8: I take time out for others.
question.e9: I don't mind being the center of attention.
question.o9: I spend time reflecting on things.
question.a10: I make people feel at ease.
question.c10: I am exacting in my work.
question.mbti1: You regularly make new friends and enjoy interacting with large groups.
question.mbti2: You feel exhausted after spending time in highly stimulating social environments.
question.mbti3: At social events, you rarely try to introduce yourself to new people and mostly talk to the ones you already know.
question.mbti4: You usually initiate conversations and don't mind being the center of attention.
question.mbti5: You prefer quiet, intimate environments over loud, bustling ones.
question.mbti6: You process your thoughts by talking them out loud with others.
question.mbti7: You often spend time exploring unrealistic yet intriguing ideas.
question.mbti8: You prefer to rely on concrete facts and direct experience rather than abstract theories.
question.mbti9: You are more of a detail-oriented person than a "big picture" person.
question.mbti10: You often wonder how things are connected and look for underlying meanings or patterns.
question.mbti11: You prefer instructions to be extremely specific and step-by-step.
question.mbti12: You are drawn to creative, open-ended projects rather than highly structured tasks.
question.mbti13: When making a decision, you prioritize objective logic over how it affects people's emotions.
question.mbti14: You are highly empathetic and easily absorb the emotions of those around you.
question.mbti15: You believe that objective truth should be more important than people's sensitivities.
question.mbti16: You often let your heart guide your decisions rather than your head.
question.mbti17: In an argument, you care more about being factually correct than keeping the peace.
question.mbti18: You prioritize social harmony and try to avoid conflict whenever possible.
question.mbti19: You prefer to have a detailed plan before starting a project or going on a trip.
question.mbti20: You often leave things until the last minute and thrive under the pressure of a deadline.
question.mbti21: You like to keep your options open rather than committing to a strict schedule.
question.mbti22: You feel more comfortable when things are decided, settled, and finalized.
question.mbti23: Your workspace and living areas are highly organized and tidy.
question.mbti24: You prefer to adapt to situations as they happen rather than planning ahead.
question.cd1: I often assume that others are reacting negatively to me, even when there is no definite evidence.
question.cd2: I tend to predict that things will turn out badly, treating my prediction as a fact.
question.cd3: If I fall short of perfection, I view myself as a total failure.
question.cd4: I often dwell on a single negative detail, ignoring all the positive things that happened.
question.cd5: When something good happens, I tell myself it doesn't count or was just luck.
question.cd6: I exaggerate the importance of my mistakes and minimize the importance of my achievements.
question.cd7: I assume that my negative emotions reflect the way things really are ("I feel it, therefore it must be true").
question.cd8: I frequently use words like "should," "must," or "ought" to motivate myself or judge others.
question.cd9: I attach a negative label to myself or others based on a single mistake (e.g., "I'm a loser").
question.cd10: I blame myself for things that are not entirely under my control.
question.cd11: I view a single negative event as a never-ending pattern of defeat.
question.cd12: I believe I know what others are thinking about me without asking them.
question.cd13: I feel that if I am not completely successful, I am completely worthless.
question.cd14: I hold myself responsible for other people's feelings or behaviors.
question.cd15: I expect disaster to strike, no matter what.
mbti.types.INTJ.title: The Architect
mbti.types.INTJ.description: Imaginative and strategic thinkers, with a plan for everything. INTJs are highly analytical, objective, and logical. They value knowledge, competence, and structure, often seeing the world as a complex system to be optimized.
mbti.types.INTJ.weaknesses: Can be overly critical or arrogant
Struggle with highly structured environments they didn't create
mbti.types.INTP.title: The Logician
mbti.types.INTP.description: Innovative inventors with an unquenchable thirst for knowledge. INTPs are brilliant theoreticians who love exploring abstract concepts. They are driven by a desire to understand how the universe works at a fundamental level.
mbti.types.INTP.weaknesses: Condescending to those they deem illogical
mbti.types.ENTJ.title: The Commander
mbti.types.ENTJ.description: Bold, imaginative, and strong-willed leaders, always finding a way—or making one. ENTJs are natural-born leaders who project authority and confidence. They are highly efficient and driven to achieve their goals.
mbti.types.ENTP.title: The Debater
mbti.types.ENTP.description: Smart and curious thinkers who cannot resist an intellectual challenge. ENTPs are the ultimate devil's advocates, thriving on the process of shredding arguments and beliefs to see what's left.
mbti.types.ENTP.weaknesses: Find it difficult to focus on practical matters
mbti.types.INFJ.title: The Advocate
mbti.types.INFJ.description: Quiet and mystical, yet very inspiring and tireless idealists. INFJs are driven by a deep sense of idealism and morality, but they are not idle dreamers—they take concrete steps to realize their goals and make a lasting positive impact.
mbti.types.INFJ.weaknesses: Always need to have a cause
mbti.types.INFP.title: The Mediator
mbti.types.INFP.description: Poetic, kind, and altruistic people, always eager to help a good cause. INFPs are true idealists, always looking for the hint of good in even the worst of people and events, searching for ways to make things better.
mbti.types.INFP.weaknesses: Dislike dealing with data and hard facts
mbti.types.ENFJ.title: The Protagonist
mbti.types.ENFJ.description: Charismatic and inspiring leaders, able to mesmerize their listeners. ENFJs are natural-born leaders, full of passion and charisma. They radiate authenticity, concern, and altruism, unafraid to stand up and speak when they feel something needs to be said.
mbti.types.ENFP.title: The Campaigner
mbti.types.ENFP.description: Enthusiastic, creative, and sociable free spirits, who can always find a reason to smile. ENFPs are fiercely independent, and much more than stability and security, they crave creativity and freedom.
mbti.types.ISTJ.title: The Logistician
mbti.types.ISTJ.description: Practical and fact-minded individuals, whose reliability cannot be doubted. ISTJs are the backbone of many societies, utilizing their time and energy to complete tasks with accuracy and patience.
mbti.types.ISFJ.title: The Defender
mbti.types.ISFJ.description: Very dedicated and warm protectors, always ready to defend their loved ones. ISFJs are true altruists, meeting kindness with kindness-in-excess and engaging the work and people they believe in with enthusiasm and generosity.
mbti.types.ESTJ.title: The Executive
mbti.types.ESTJ.description: Excellent administrators, unsurpassed at managing things—or people. ESTJs represent tradition and order, utilizing their understanding of what is right, wrong, and socially acceptable to bring families and communities together.
mbti.types.ESTJ.weaknesses: Focus too much on social status
mbti.types.ESFJ.title: The Consul
mbti.types.ESFJ.description: Extraordinarily caring, social, and popular people, always eager to help. ESFJs are the cheerleaders and the quarterbacks, setting the tone, taking the spotlight, and leading their teams forward to victory and fame.
mbti.types.ISTP.title: The Virtuoso
mbti.types.ISTP.description: Bold and practical experimenters, masters of all kinds of tools. ISTPs love to explore with their hands and their eyes, touching and examining the world around them with cool rationalism and spirited curiosity.
mbti.types.ISFP.title: The Adventurer
mbti.types.ISFP.description: Flexible and charming artists, always ready to explore and experience something new. ISFPs live in a colorful, sensual world, inspired by connections with people and ideas.
mbti.types.ESTP.title: The Entrepreneur
mbti.types.ESTP.description: Smart, energetic, and very perceptive people, who truly enjoy living on the edge. ESTPs always have an impact on their immediate surroundings—the best way to spot them at a party is to look for the whirling eddy of people flitting about them as they move from group to group.
mbti.types.ESFP.title: The Entertainer
mbti.types.ESFP.description: Spontaneous, energetic, and enthusiastic people—life is never boring around them. ESFPs get caught up in the excitement of the moment, and want everyone else to feel that way, too.
bigFive.traits.O.name: Openness to Experience
bigFive.traits.O.description: Measures your willingness to try new things, your imagination, and your intellectual curiosity.
bigFive.traits.O.low: You tend to be pragmatic, data-driven, and prefer routine over novelty. You likely value tradition and the "tried-and-true" methods over abstract or theoretical concepts. While this makes you reliable and grounded, you might sometimes miss out on creative solutions.
bigFive.traits.O.medium: You strike a balance between the practical and the abstract. You are open to new experiences when they make sense, but you don't seek novelty just for the sake of it. You appreciate both tradition and innovation.
bigFive.traits.O.high: You are highly imaginative, curious, and open-minded. You likely enjoy art, abstract concepts, and exploring new ideas or places. You are a creative thinker who thrives on variety, though you might sometimes struggle with highly repetitive or mundane tasks.
bigFive.traits.O.implications: In the workplace, high Openness correlates with success in creative or strategic roles, while low Openness excels in operational, detail-oriented roles requiring consistency.
bigFive.traits.C.name: Conscientiousness
bigFive.traits.C.description: Measures your reliability, organization, and goal-directed behavior.
bigFive.traits.C.low: You are flexible, spontaneous, and adaptable. You likely prefer to "go with the flow" rather than stick to a rigid schedule. While this makes you great at handling sudden changes, you might struggle with long-term planning, deadlines, or maintaining deep focus on tedious tasks.
bigFive.traits.C.medium: You are reasonably organized but not obsessive about it. You can meet deadlines and keep things in order, but you also know when to relax your standards and be spontaneous.
bigFive.traits.C.high: You are highly organized, disciplined, and detail-oriented. You prefer planned rather than spontaneous behavior. You are reliable and driven by a strong sense of duty. However, you might sometimes be perceived as stubborn or overly perfectionistic.
bigFive.traits.C.implications: Conscientiousness is the strongest predictor of occupational performance across all job types. High scorers are excellent at execution, while lower scorers may need external structures to stay on track.
bigFive.traits.E.name: Extraversion
bigFive.traits.E.description: Measures your sociability, assertiveness, and the extent to which you draw energy from social interactions.
bigFive.traits.E.low: You are introverted. You draw energy from solitary activities or small, intimate gatherings. You likely prefer listening over speaking in large groups and think before you act. You are not necessarily anti-social, but social stimulation drains your battery faster.
bigFive.traits.E.medium: You are an ambivert. You enjoy social interaction but also require alone time to recharge. You can adapt to being the center of attention or staying in the background depending on the context and your current energy levels.
bigFive.traits.E.high: You are highly extraverted. You draw energy from being around others, enjoy being the center of attention, and are likely assertive and talkative. You thrive in fast-paced, highly social environments and tend to act quickly.
bigFive.traits.E.implications: Extraverts often excel in sales, leadership, and public-facing roles. Introverts often excel in roles requiring deep, uninterrupted focus, analytical thinking, and one-on-one relationship building.
bigFive.traits.A.name: Agreeableness
bigFive.traits.A.description: Measures your tendency to be compassionate, cooperative, and trusting toward others.
bigFive.traits.A.low: You are competitive, critical, and skeptical. You prioritize objective truth and your own interests over group harmony. While this can make you an excellent negotiator or critical thinker who isn't afraid to challenge the status quo, you might sometimes be perceived as blunt or uncooperative.
bigFive.traits.A.medium: You are generally warm and cooperative, but you know how to stand your ground when necessary. You value harmony but won't compromise your core boundaries just to please others.
bigFive.traits.A.high: You are highly empathetic, altruistic, and cooperative. You value social harmony and are likely very trusting and forgiving. You are a natural team player and caregiver. However, you might sometimes struggle to assert your own needs or engage in necessary conflict.
bigFive.traits.A.implications: High agreeableness is excellent for caregiving, teaching, and collaborative roles. Low agreeableness can be advantageous in highly competitive environments, negotiations, or roles requiring objective, tough decisions.
bigFive.traits.N.name: Neuroticism (Emotional Stability)
bigFive.traits.N.description: Measures your tendency to experience negative emotions like anxiety, anger, or depression in response to stress.
bigFive.traits.N.low: You are emotionally stable, resilient, and calm under pressure. You rarely experience severe mood swings and tend to bounce back quickly from setbacks. You handle stress well, though you might sometimes under-react to genuine threats.
bigFive.traits.N.medium: You experience a normal range of human emotions. You get stressed by genuinely stressful situations but generally maintain your composure. You have a balanced emotional reactivity.
bigFive.traits.N.high: You are highly sensitive to stress and environmental stimuli. You likely experience emotions intensely and may frequently feel anxious, frustrated, or sad. While this means you might struggle with stress management, it also often correlates with high empathy, vigilance, and a deep capacity for emotional understanding.
bigFive.traits.N.implications: Lower neuroticism is generally beneficial for high-stress jobs. However, higher neuroticism can be an asset in roles requiring high vigilance, risk assessment, or deep emotional attunement to others.
cbt.distortions.MindReading.name: Mind Reading
cbt.distortions.MindReading.description: Assuming you know what people think without having sufficient evidence of their thoughts.
cbt.distortions.MindReading.implications: This can lead to unnecessary social anxiety, defensiveness, and strained relationships. Try asking people directly instead of assuming.
cbt.distortions.FortuneTelling.name: Fortune Telling
cbt.distortions.FortuneTelling.descripti: Predicting the future negatively: expecting that things will turn out badly and feeling convinced that your prediction is an already-established fact.
cbt.distortions.FortuneTelling.implicati: This creates a self-fulfilling prophecy of anxiety and avoidance. Remember that the future is uncertain and past failures do not guarantee future ones.
cbt.distortions.AllorNothingThinking.des: Viewing situations in only two categories instead of on a continuum (e.g., if a performance falls short of perfect, you see yourself as a total failure).
cbt.distortions.AllorNothingThinking.imp: This perfectionistic mindset can lead to severe procrastination, low self-esteem, and giving up easily. Try to find the "gray areas" in situations.
cbt.distortions.MentalFilter.name: Mental Filter
cbt.distortions.MentalFilter.description: Dwelling on a single negative detail exclusively so that your vision of all reality becomes darkened.
cbt.distortions.MentalFilter.implication: This prevents you from enjoying successes and keeps you stuck in a negative mood. Practice actively listing positive events to balance your perspective.
cbt.distortions.DiscountingthePositive.d: Unreasonably telling yourself that positive experiences, deeds, or qualities do not count.
cbt.distortions.DiscountingthePositive.i: This strips the joy from your achievements and maintains a negative self-image despite evidence to the contrary. Accept compliments with a simple "thank you."
cbt.distortions.MagnificationMinimizatio: Exaggerating the importance of things (such as your goof-up) or inappropriately shrinking things until they appear tiny (your own desirable qualities).
This distorts your self-evaluation. Try to look at your mistakes and successes through the eyes of a neutral observer.
cbt.distortions.EmotionalReasoning.name: Emotional Reasoning
cbt.distortions.EmotionalReasoning.descr: Assuming that because you feel a certain way, it must be true (e.g., "I feel like an idiot, so I must be one").
cbt.distortions.EmotionalReasoning.impli: Emotions are not facts. They are reactions to your thoughts. When you feel overwhelmed, try to identify the thought causing the emotion and test its validity.
cbt.distortions.ShouldStatements.name: Should Statements
cbt.distortions.ShouldStatements.descrip: Trying to motivate yourself with "shoulds" and "shouldn'ts," as if you had to be whipped and punished before you could be expected to do anything.
cbt.distortions.ShouldStatements.implica: This leads to guilt and frustration. When directed at others, it leads to anger and resentment. Try replacing "should" with "prefer" or "would like to."
cbt.distortions.Labeling.name: Labeling
cbt.distortions.Labeling.description: An extreme form of overgeneralization. Instead of describing your error, you attach a negative label to yourself.
cbt.distortions.Labeling.implications: Labels are highly irrational because you are not your behavior. You are a human being who makes mistakes. Focus on the specific behavior, not the person.
cbt.distortions.Personalization.name: Personalization
cbt.distortions.Personalization.descript: Seeing yourself as the cause of some negative external event which in fact you were not primarily responsible for.
cbt.distortions.Personalization.implicat: This leads to immense guilt and a crushing sense of responsibility. Recognize that other people's actions are their own responsibility, not yours.
cbt.distortions.Overgeneralization.name: Overgeneralization
cbt.distortions.Overgeneralization.descr: Seeing a single negative event as a never-ending pattern of defeat.
cbt.distortions.Overgeneralization.impli: This turns a temporary setback into a permanent state. Use precise language (e.g., "I failed this specific test" rather than "I always fail").
